text: In this video, we will present the laparoscopic repair of left para duodenal hernia.
Para duodenal hernia is a type of internal abdominal hernias and occurs when the small bowel herniates into the left or right para duodenal fossa.
The clinical manifestation can be ranged from recurrent abdominal pain attacks to acute intestinal obstruction with or without strangulation.
Although para duodenal hernias have unique computed tomography appearance, the diagnosis of para duodenal hernias can be missed in emergency settings.
The only treatment option is surgery.
The surgeon reduces the herniated small bowel loops and closes the hernia orifice during the laparoscopic repair of a left para duodenal hernia.
In the majority of previous reports, open approach was used for hernia repair, however laparoscopic repair is also possible when the diagnosis is made preoperatively.
The presented case is a twenty-two year old male and admitted to the emergency department with abdominal pain and vomiting.
The patient expressed several previous visits to emergency departments owing to similar complaints and no definite diagnosis was established.
Epigastric distension was the only finding in physical examination and no peritonitis finding was observed.
On computer tomography evaluation.
A sac-like cluster of small bowel loops is visually identified within the hernia sac, located on the left side of the fourth part of the duodenum and posterior to the transverse colon.
The surgeon observes fluid accumulation and congestion within the hernia sac containing small bowel loops.
The diagnosis was confirmed as left para-duodenal hernia with incarcerated small bowel loops.
During the surgery, the transverse mesocolon is retracted caudally, revealing the underlying structures and facilitating access to the hernia sac containing small bowel loops.
The surgeon retracts the small bowel, revealing a hernia orifice located on the left side of the duodenal-jejunal junction.
The surgeon reduces the small bowel loops from the hernia sac into the peritoneal cavity, reaching the area just after the fourth part of the duodenum.
The surgical field shows no signs of ischemia in the small bowel loops within the left para-duodenal hernia sac.
The surgeon evaluates the hernia sac, revealing a small amount of serohemorrhagic fluid within the cavity.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic suturing device to close the hernia orifice at the left side of the duodenal-jejunal junction with a non-absorbable suture.
The surgeon uses the purse string method to close the hernia orifice entirely, taking serosal bites from the jejunal segment.
The surgeon completes the operation after verifying the closure of the hernia orifice.
After surgery, patient was discharged on post-operative third day without any complication and he has not experienced any complaints in two-year follow-up period.
As a conclusion, laparoscopic surgery can be used as a feasible and safe approach in the treatment of left para-duodenal hernia.
